items.abstract: <jats:p>The rise of new entrants in content-streaming markets has intensified competition for incumbents such as Netflix, prompting a reassessment of pricing and content strategies. This study analyzes an incumbent platform offering both exclusive and nonexclusive content facing an entrant that provides only nonexclusive content under subscription or usage-based pricing. We develop an analytical framework incorporating consumer heterogeneity, consumption intensity, content acquisition costs, and entrant pricing. Our findings show that incumbents do not always differentiate their pricing and may match the entrant’s pricing strategy under certain conditions. Entry depends on a cost threshold shaped by consumer valuation and incumbent response, and higher valuation does not always increase entry incentives, as incumbents can strategically deter entry. We also find that entrants may invest more in content when acquisition costs and exclusivity appreciation are low, and incumbents do not always enjoy a positional advantage, as profitability depends on market conditions. Overall, the study provides actionable insights, highlighting the need for platforms to align pricing and content strategies with consumer behavior, competitive dynamics, and content characteristics in evolving streaming markets.</jats:p>
title: Response to Entry in Streaming Markets: A Game-Theoretic Model

items.abstract:
<jats:p>Practice and Policy Oriented Abstract for “Ecosystem Competition: Platforms, Subsidiary Markets and Multihoming in the Videogames Industry”</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>This study examines how royalty-bearing game sales and multi-platform publishing reshape competition in the videogame console industry. By modeling consoles, publishers, and gamers as part of an interconnected ecosystem rather than a simple two-sided market, the analysis shows that downstream game revenues can intensify, rather than soften, platform competition. When consoles expect to earn royalties from later game purchases, they have stronger incentives to lower hardware prices to attract gamers, while shifting more of the revenue burden to publishers through higher licensing fees under royalty-based contracts. The study also finds that when publishers release games on multiple platforms, gamer subsidies deepen, licensing fees rise, and multihoming may cease to benefit platforms beyond a threshold, overturning a common assumption in platform strategy.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>For practice, these findings suggest that platform managers should evaluate pricing, exclusivity, and revenue-sharing decisions at the ecosystem level, not in isolation. For policy, the results imply that regulators and industry observers should expect royalty structures and content distribution rules to affect consumer prices, publisher bargaining power, and market competition in digital ecosystems more broadly.</jats:p>
title: Ecosystem Competition: Platforms, Subsidiary Markets and Multihoming in the Videogames Industry

affiliation.name: Goizueta Business School, Emory University, Atlanta, Georgia 30322
Mays Business School, Texas A&M University, College Station, Texas 77843

items.abstract: <jats:p>As AI becomes increasingly embedded in multiplayer online games, AI-controlled players are frequently deployed as opponents to human players. Whereas this practice is becoming increasingly common, its impact on player engagement, social interactions, and the broader game ecosystem remains unclear. Our study investigates the effects of introducing AI players using a natural experiment from a popular multiplayer online game. We find that AI players significantly increase player engagement and encourage players to team up with friends more frequently. These effects are driven by enhanced self-efficacy and perceived team responsibility. Moreover, the positive effects are particularly strong among novice players and among players who previously preferred teaming up with strangers. Our findings offer valuable insights for game developers seeking to enhance player engagement and foster social interaction, highlighting the potential of carefully designed AI participants to strengthen online communities and improve player retention.</jats:p>
title: Interlopers or Catalysts? Dissecting the Impact of Incorporating AI Players on Multiplayer Online Games

affiliation.name: Costello College of Business, George Mason University, Fairfax, Virginia 22030
